content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 27, 1995

Patricia J. Randolph
Deputy County Counsel
County of Kern
Administrative Center
1115 Truxtun Avenue
Bakersfield, CA 93301

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-95-045

Dear Ms. Randolph:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the Kern County Employees' Retirement Association (the
KCERA) regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). We are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Are any employees of the various investment management
firms that contract with the KCERA required to file statements of
economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of
interest code of the KCERA?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 2


     2. Are any employees of Wilshire Associates Inc. (the
investment consultant to the KCERA) required to file statements of
economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of
interest code of the KCERA?

     3. What are the county's legal obligations with respect to
ensuring that individuals file statements of economic interests?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. Section 87200, as interpreted by Regulation 18720(b)(3),
would not apply to the employees of the investment management
firms. However, since the employees of the investment managers
make decisions regarding the investment of public funds that would
otherwise have to be made by a public agency, they are
"consultants." Thus, the employees of the investment managers who
are responsible for investing the funds of the KCERA are public
officials with filing obligations pursuant to the KCERA's conflict
of interest code.

     2. You stated that the employees of Wilshire do not direct
the investment of public moneys, formulate or approve the
investment policies of the KCERA, approve or establish guidelines
for asset allocations, or approve investment transactions, as set
forth in the definition of "management of public investments" in
Regulation 18720(a)(3).

     However, individuals who serve on an ongoing basis with an
agency and perform the same or substantially all the duties for
the agency that would be performed by an individual whose
position would be listed in the agency's conflict of interest code
are "consultants." Thus, the employees of Wilshire Associates
Inc. that are responsible for providing these services to the
KCERA are "consultants" and must file statements of economic
interests pursuant to the conflict of interest code of the KCERA.

     3. The county's conflict of interest code must: (1) provide
reasonable assurance that all potential conflicts of interest will
be disclosed or prevented; (2) provide to each affected person a
clear and specific statement of his or her duties under the
conflict of interest code; and (3) adequately differentiate
between designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.

     In addition to other duties, filing officers must:
(1) determine whether required documents have been filed;
(2) determine whether the documents conform with the requirements
of the act; (3) notify persons who have failed to file; and
(4) report apparent violations.

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 3


FACTS

The Board of Retirement (the "board") for the Kern County
Employees' Retirement Association is comprised of nine members and
an alternate.  The KCERA board is charged with the responsibility
of managing the retirement system, including investment of the
retirement fund.  The board has the sole authority to develop and
execute the investment policy.  The investments are based on a
comprehensive financial plan that takes into consideration various
alternative investment mixes, their resulting risk and return
levels, and the current and projected liabilities of the plan.
Based on a review of the financial plan, the board will adopt
asset allocation ranges which will express the minimum and maximum
acceptable level of investment for each asset class.

The Investment Managers

The KCERA delegates the investment management of assets to
investment management firms ("investment managers").  The
investment managers are given discretion to manage the assets
within the context of established guidelines set by the KCERA
board.  The investment managers have full discretion to direct and
manage the investment and reinvestment of assets in accordance
with applicable federal and state statutes and regulations.  The
investment managers may also direct trades to specific brokers,
and choose fee arrangements with the brokers (broker fees vary
from broker to broker).  In addition, the investment managers may
accept higher fees and accept research or other services for the
benefit of the plan participants in exchange.

The KCERA prohibits the investment managers from purchasing
nonnegotiable securities, short sales, transactions on margin, use
of leverage and use of options other than directed by the KCERA
board.  In addition, the investment manager under contract to the
KCERA must advise the KCERA within 24 hours if at any time there
is:

1.  A significant change in investment philosophy;

2.  A loss of one or more key management personnel;

3.  A new portfolio manager on the KCERA's account;

4.  A change in ownership of the firm;

5.  A change in the ownership structure of the firm; or

6.  Any occurrence which might potentially impact the
management, professionalism, integrity, organizational structure
or financial position of the management firm.

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 4


     Finally, pursuant to the agreement, the investment managers
agree that all of its officers, employees and agents who
participate in making investment decisions with respect to the
fund will comply with applicable state conflict-of-interest
disclosure requirements and those of the KCERA.

     In the Investment Goals, Objectives and Policies Manual
(revised November 1993) that you submitted with your request for
advice, listed as investment managers responsible for a portion of
the assets of the KCERA are: Equitable Capital, Fisher
Investments, Newbold's Asset Management, Parametric Portfolio
Associates Inc., Sun Bank Investment Management Group, Wells Fargo
Investment Advisors, UBS Phillips & Drew International Investment,
Wardley Investment Services International, Wells Fargo Nikko
Investment Advisors, Mellon Bond Associates, Fiduciary Trust
Company, WorldInvest Limited, Fiduciary Trust Company
International, Grubb & Ellis Company, and FFCA International
Advisors. Each investor a portion of the KCERA's assets and each
is responsible for a different aspect of the KCERA's investment
plan.

Wilshire Associates Incorporated (Wilshire)

     Wilshire is an investment consulting firm retained by the
KCERA to assist the retirement administrator. The employees of
Wilshire do not make any decisions with respect to the investment
of funds. According to the agreement between Wilshire and the
KCERA, the duties of Wilshire's employees are as follows:

     1.  Annual review of the retirement fund financial condition;

     2.  Annual review of the asset allocation plan;

     3.  Review of investment policies and guidelines annually or
more often at the KCERA board's request;

     4.  Annual review of investment structure of the retirement
fund;

     5.  Provide assistance to the board in selection of
investment managers at the board's request;

     6.  Provide quarterly and annual performance reports
monitoring performance of investment managers and overall
performance of the retirement fund;

     7.  Provide "universe" comparisons of the retirement fund's
performance as compared with other public pension systems;

     8.  Provide "universe" comperisons of´the performance of the
investment managers as compared with other investment managers
managing the same class of asset and with similar investment
philosophies;

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 5


     9.  Assist the board in negotiating fees for investment
managers, whether performance-based or otherwise;

     10.  Monitor transactions made by investment managers on a
semiannual basis for the purpose of determining the amount of
commissions paid and whether transactions are accomplished in a
manner consistent with the board's investment policies and
guidelines;

     11.  Document facts and analysis which form the basis of
advice provided to the board;

     12.  Attend all board meetings in which investments and
issues relating to investments are to be discussed and such other
meetings (including meetings by standing committees of the board)
where advice and input is requested; and,

     13.  Perform such other services and provide reports on such
other investment issues that may be requested by the board or
staff from time to time.

     When it is necessary and appropriate, the employees of
Wilshire confer and coordinate with the KCERA's actuaries in order
to advise the KCERA with respect to future plan obligations and an
appropriate investment plan which will enable the KCERA to meet
those obligations.  You stated that Wilshire has no authority over
the conduct of the investment managers.

     Currently, all members of the KCERA board, all natural
persons who advise the board in the capacity of investment counsel
(including employees of Wilshire and the investment managers), and
the retirement administrator are designated in the conflict of
interest code of the KCERA.  They must disclose investments in,
and income from, business entities which are or may foreseeably be
invested in by the KCERA and investments in, and income from,
persons or business entities engaged in buying and selling
securities for the account of the KCERA.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California
by initiative in 1974.  The purpose for the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)  In furtherance of this goal, the Act requires
that assets and income of public officials which may be materially
affected by their official actions should be fully disclosed and
in appropriate circumstances the officials should be disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.
(Section 81002(c).)

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 6

1.  Other Public Officials who Manage Public Investments

You have asked whether any employees of Wilshire or your
investment managers qualify as filers under Section 87200 and
Regulation 18720. Regulation 18720(b) provides that "other public
officials who manage public investments," as that term is used in
Section 87200, means:

(1)  Members of boards and commissions,
including pension and retirement boards or
commissions, or of committees thereof, who exercise
responsibility for the management of public
investments;

(2)  High-level officers and employees of
public agencies who exercise primary responsibility
for the management of public investments, such as
chief or principal investment officers or chief
financial managers. This category shall not
include officers and employees who work under the
supervision of the chief or principal investment
officers or the chief financial managers; and

(3)  Individuals who, pursuant to a contract
with a state or local government agency, perform
the same or substantially all the same functions
that would otherwise be performed by the public
officials described in subdivision (b)(2) above..

Subdivision (b)(1) applies to members of the board itself.
As you noted, the board members exercise responsibility for the
management of public investments³ and therefore qualify as "other
public officials who manage public investments," as that term is
used in Section 87200.⁴

___________________________
³   Regulation 18720(a)(3) defines the "management of public
investments" as the following nonministerial functions: directing
the investment of public moneys; formulating or approving
investment policies; approving or establishing guidelines for
asset allocations; or approving investment transactions.
⁴   "Public investments" means the investment of public moneys in
real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the
production of revenue or other financial return. (Regulation
18720(a)(1).) "Public moneys" means all moneys belonging to,
received by, or held by, the state, or any city, county, town,
district, or public agency therein, or by an officer thereof
acting in his or her official capacity, and includes the proceeds
of all bonds and other evidences of indebtedness, trust funds held
by public pension and retirement systems, deferred compensation
funds held for investment by public agencies, and public moneys
held by a financial institution under a trust indenture to which a
public agency is a party. (Regulation 18720(a)(2).)

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 7


Subdivision (b)(2) applies to high-level officers and
employees of the agency who exercise primary responsibility for
the management of public investments, such as chief or principal
investment officers or chief financial managers.

Regulation 18720(b)(3) provides that individuals who,
pursuant to a contract with a state or local government agency,
perform the same or substantially all the same functions that
would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in
subdivision (b)(2) qualify as "other public officials who manage
public investments." Thus, this provision would apply to high-
level officers and employees of the contracting firm who exercise
primary responsibility for the management of public investments,
such as chief or principal investment officers or chief financial
managers.

Investment Managers: According to the material you provided in
connection with your request for advice, the KCERA delegates the
investment management of assets to the investment managers. This
would constitute the "management of public investments" as defined
in Regulation 18720(a)(3).

Moreover, within the confines of guidelines established by
the board of the KCERA, the investment managers have full
discretion to direct and manage the investment and reinvestment of
assets and may direct trades to specific brokers and receive
research or other services which are of benefit to plan
participants in the management of the plan's assets.

However, it does not appear from your facts that any of the
investment managers act as chief or principal investment officers
or chief financial managers for the KCERA. It appears that
each of the 15 investment managers in question has their authority
limited to a share of the KCERA's assets and to the types of
investments the manager has contracted to utilize, and that there
is no individual fulfilling this chief investment manager
function. Thus, the investment managers would not fall within the
scope of Regulation 18720.

Wilshire: According to your facts, the employees of Wilshire do
not direct the investment of public moneys into specific
securities, formulate or approve the investment policies of the
KCERA, approve or establish guidelines for asset allocations, or
approve investment transactions, as set forth in the definition of
the "management of public investments" in Regulation 18720(a)(3).
Thus, the employees of Wilshire would not be subject to Section
87200, as interpreted by Regulation 18720.5

_________________________
5 As noted previously, Commission advice is based on the facts
provided by the requestor. If Wilshire does, in fact, perform
these functions, you should contact us for further advice.

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 8


2.  Consultants

     Of course, employees of these firms may still be
"consultants" and have filing obligations pursuant to the conflict
of interest code of the KCERA.  The comment to Regulation 18720
provides that individuals not coming within the definition
contained in Regulation 18720(b) may be subject to the conflict-
of-interest disclosure requirements of their agency's conflict of
interest code.  If this is the case, those individuals must file a
statement of economic interests (Form 730).  (See also, Regulation
18700(a) and Section 82019.)

     In April 1994, Regulation 18700(a)(2) was amended to provide
a new definition of "consultant."  Under the new definition, an
individual is a consultant if, pursuant to a contract, the
individual:

     (A)  Makes a governmental decision whether to:

          (i)  Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;

          (ii)  Adopt or enforce a law;

          (iii)  Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke
     any permit, license, application, certificate,
     approval, order, or similar authorization or
     entitlement;

          (iv)  Authorize the agency to enter into,
     modify, or renew a contract provided it is the
     type of contract which requires agency
     approval;

          (v)  Grant agency approval to a contract
     which requires agency approval and in which
     the agency is a party or to the specifications
     for such a contract;

          (vi)  Grant agency approval to a plan,
     design, report, study, or similar item;

          (vii)  Adopt, or grant agency approval
     of, policies, standards, or guidelines for the
     agency, or for any subdivision thereof; or

     (B)  Serves in a staff capacity with the
agency and in that capacity performs the same or
substantially all the same duties for the agency
that would otherwise be performed by an individual
holding a position specified in the agency's
Conflict of Interest Code.

Investment Managers:  While the investment managers are not within
the scope of Regulation 18720, they are clearly "consultants"

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 9


under the definition in Regulation 18700. According to the
agreement, the investment managers make decisions concerning the
investment of public funds and may direct trades to specific
brokers and agree to a broker's fee. They may also accept higher
fees and accept research or other services from the broker in
exchange.6 These are decisions that would be customarily made by
the board itself or its employees. Thus, in essence, the power of
the board to enter into investment contracts has been delegated to
the investment managers. For these reasons, the employees of the
investment managers who provide services to the KCERA are
currently designated in the conflict of interest code and should
continue to be designated as consultants and subject to the
provisions of the Act.

Of course, not all employees of the investment managers are
considered consultants. However, those persons with influence
over the investment decisions pertaining to the KCERA funds would
appear to be consultants under Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Wilshire: According to your facts, the employees of Wilshire do
not make any of the decisions set forth in subdivision (a)(2)(A)
above. However, it appears that Wilshire employees may serve in a
staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity perform the
same or substantially all the same duties for the agency that
would otherwise be performed by an individual holding a position
specified in the agency's conflict of interest code.

The test established in Regulation 18700(a)(2)(B) is a two-
fold test, both of which must be satisfied for an individual to
qualify as a consultant.

The first prong, the 'staff capacity' language, eliminates
in most cases from the scope of the regulation those individuals
who work on one project or a limited range of projects for an
agency. In addition, there is a temporal element to the
qualifier. (See March 28, 1994 memorandum to the Commission
regarding Regulation 18700, pages 3 and 4.)

Since the adoption of amended Regulation 18700(a)(2), we have
had one opportunity to interpret the provision. In the Lyion
Advice Letter, No. A-94-353, we concluded that employees of firms

6 The sample agreement submitted with your request provides that
"the Manager, acting as KCERA's agent and attorney-in-fact, shall
have discretionary investment authority, without obtaining the
consent of or consulting with the Board or any other person; to
issue to brokers instructions to sell, and otherwise trade in or
deal with any asset in the Account; to sell to any person any
assets in the Account; to instruct any trustee or custodian of any
asset of the Account to deliver securities or other assets sold,
exchanged, or otherwise disposed of from the Account and generally
to perform any other act necessary or proper to enable Manager to
carry out its obligations...."

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 10


that were retained to assist the city in a general plan update did
not meet the requirements of Regulation 18700(a)(2)(B). While the
contractor had primary responsibility for the general plan
preparation, the contractor was only making recommendations
limited to the specific project. Thus, we advised that none of
the individuals served in a staff capacity with the agency or
performed the same or substantially all the same duties for the
agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual
designated in the agency's conflict of interest code.

     Conversely, pursuant to the agreement with Wilshire,
employees of the firm attend all board meetings in which
investments and issues relating to investments are to be discussed
and such other meetings (including meetings by standing committees
of the KCERA board) at which advice and input is requested. In
addition, the employees of Wilshire are required to perform such
other services and provide reports on such other investment issues
that may be requested by the KCERA board or staff from time to
time. Unlike in the Lyon letter, it appears that the employees
of Wilshire serve on an ongoing basis as staff for the KCERA and
in that capacity participate in the making of all investment
decisions.

     "The second prong of the test adds an additional condition:
the tasks of the quasi-staff member over this period of time must
be substantially the same as one of the individuals whose position
at the agency is described in the Conflict of Interest Code."
(See March 28, 1994 memorandum to the Commission regarding
Regulation 18700, page 4.)

     Section 87300 provides:

          Every agency shall adopt and promulgate a
     Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the
     provisions of this article. A Conflict of Interest
     Code shall have the force of law and any violation
     of a Conflict of Interest Code by a designated
     employee shall be deemed a violation of this
     chapter.

     Section 87302 requires that every conflict of interest code
must specifically enumerate the positions within the agency which
involve the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest.

     A conflict of interest code is necessarily a fluid document
with positions being added, amended and deleted on a periodic
basis. In fact, Section 87306.5 requires that the code reviewing
body must have the conflict of interest code reviewed every two
years to amend its provisions and positions designated therein.
Moreover, Section 87309 prohibits a code reviewing body from
approving a conflict of interest code or amendment to a conflict
of interest code if it fails to do any of the following:

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 11


     (a)  Provide reasonable assurance that all
foreseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented;

     (b)  Provide to each affected person a clear
and specific statement of his duties under the
Code; or

     (c)  Adequately differentiate between
designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.

     Wilshire employees prepare and present reports or opinions
which require the exercise of judgment on their part (as set forth
in Regulation 18700(c)(2)(B)) and for the purpose of influencing a
governmental decision referenced in subdivision (a)(2)(A)--namely,
board decisions to enter into, modify, or renew contracts with
investment managers, as well as decisions regarding policies,
standards, and guidelines for the agency.  (Regulation
18700(a)(2)(A)(iv), (v), (vi) and (vii).)

     These types of services constitute the "participation" in
decisions and if the services were performed by an employee
of the agency, the employee would be designated in the conflict of
interest code.7  For these reasons, the employees of Wilshire
who provide services to the KCERA are currently designated in the
conflict of interest code and should continue to be designated as
consultants and subject to the provisions of the Act.

     Disclosure Categories: We note that the current disclosure
obligations of Wilshire employees and those of the investment
managers has been limited to investments in, and income from,
business entities which are or may foreseeably be invested in by
the KCERA and investments in, and income from, persons on business
entities engaged in buying and selling securities for the account
of the KCERA.  We interpret the language in your conflict of
interest code to require disclosure of all interests in any
business entity that may cause a conflict of interest.


_________________________

7  As noted above, Section 87302 requires that every conflict of
interest code include positions within the agency that involve the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.  An
individual that advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, either by conducting research or making any
investigation for the purpose of influencing the decisionmaker or
preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally,
or in writing for the purpose of influencing a governmental
decision is participating in making a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(c).)

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 12


     Unlike those persons required to file under Regulation 18720
and who are required to disclose all economic interests, a
tailored disclosure category in the conflict of interest code is
appropriate so long as this disclosure provides reasonable
assurance that all foreseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented.

     Please note, however, that under some circumstances greater
disclosure may be necessary, consistent with section 87309. Of
course this will depend on the nature of the powers delegated to
the investment managers and the contractual limits placed on their
power to invest.

3. The County's Legal Obligations

     As noted above, a code reviewing body for the KCERA, Section
87309 provides that the county may not approve a conflict of
interest code or amendment to a conflict of interest code if it
fails to do any of the following:

          (a)  Provide reasonable assurance that all
     foreseeable potential conflict of interest
     situations will be disclosed or prevented;

          (b)  Provide to each affected person a clear
     and specific statement of his duties under the
     Code; or

          (c)  Adequately differentiate between
     designated employees with different powers and
     responsibilities.

     Section 87302 requires that every conflict of interest code
must specifically enumerate the positions within the agency which
involve the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest. In addition, the conflict of interest code shall
contain requirements that each designated employee shall file
statements disclosing reportable investments, business positions,
interests in real property and income, limited to those that may
be affected by the employee's decisionmaking.

     Moreover, pursuant to Section 81010, filing officers are
required to:

          (a)  Supply the necessary forms and manuals
     prescribed by the Commission;

          (b)  Determine whether required documents have
     been filed and, if so, whether they conform on
     their face with the requirements of this title;

Our File No. I-95-045
Page 13


     (c) Notify promptly all persons and known
committees who have failed to file a report or
statement in the form and at the time required by
this title;

     (d) Report apparent violations of this title
to the appropriate agencies; and

     (e) Compile and maintain a current list of
all reports and statements filed with this office.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                              By: John W. Wallace
                                  Counsel, Legal Division

1.
Section 87200,
as interpreted by Regulation 18720(b) (3),
would not apply to the employees of the investment management
firms.
However, since the employees of the investment managers
make decisions regarding the investment of public funds that would
otherwise have to be made by a public agency, they are
“consultants.”
Thus, the employees of the investment managers who
are responsible for investing the funds of the KCERA are public
officials with filing obligations pursuant to the KCERA’s conflict
of interest code.
2.
You stated that the employees of Wilshire do not direct
the investment of public moneys,
formulate or approve the
investment policies of the KCERA,
approve or establish guidelines
for asset allocations,
or approve investment transactions, as set
forth in the definition of “management of public investments” in
Regulation 18720(a) (3).
However,
individuals who serve on an ongoing basis with an
agency and perform the same or substantially all the same duties
for the agency that would be performed by an individual whose
position would be listed in the agency’s conflict of interest code
are “consultants.”
Thus, the employees of Wilshire Associates
Inc. that are responsible for providing these services to the
KCERA are “consultants” and must file statements of economic
interests pursuant to the conflict of interest code of the KCERA.
3.
The county’s conflict of interest code must:
(1)
provide
reasonable assurance that all potential conflicts of interest will
be disclosed or prevented;
(2) provide to each affected person a
clear and specific statement of his or her duties under the
conflict of interest code;
and
(3)
adequately differentiate
between designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.
In addition to other duties, filing officers must:
(1)
determine whether required documents have been filed;
(2)
determine whether the documents conform with the requirements
of the Act;
(3)
notify persons who have fajied to file;
and

sections.facts:
The Board of Retirement (the “board”)
for the Kern County
Employees’ Retirement Association is comprised of nine members and
an alternate.
The KCERA board is charged with the responsibility
of managing the retirement system, including investment of the
retirement fund.
The board has the sole authority to develop and
execute the investment policy.
The investments are based on a
comprehensive financial plan that takes into consideration various
alternative investment mixes, their resulting risk and return
levels,
and the current and projected liabilities of the plan.
Based on a review of the financial plan, the board will adopt
asset allocation ranges which will express the minimum and maximum
acceptable level of investment for each asset class.
The Investment Managers
The KCERA delegates the investment management of assets to
investment management firms
(“investment managers”).
The
investment managers are given discretion to manage the assets
within the context of established guidelines set by the KCERA
board.
The investment managers have full discretion to direct and
manage the investment and reinvestment of assets in accordance
with applicable federal and state statutes and regulations.
The
investment managers may also direct trades to specific brokers,
and choose fee arrangements with the brokers
(broker fees vary
from broker to broker).
In addition, the investment managers may
accept higher fees and accept research or other services for the
benefit of the plan participants in exchange.
The KCERA prohibits the investment managers from purchasing
nonnegotiable securities, short sales, transactions on margin, use
of leverage and use of options other than directed by the KCERA
board.
In addition, the investment manager under contract to the
KCERA must advise the KCERA within 24 hours if at any time there
is:
1.
A significant change in investment philosophy;
2.
A loss of one or more key management personnel;
3.
A new portfolio manager on the KCERA’s account;
4.
A change in ownership of the firm;
5.
A change in the ownership structure of the firm; or
6.
Any occurrence which might potentially impact the
management, professionalism, integrity,
organizational structure
or financial position of the management firm.


Our Page
4
Finally, pursuant to the agreement, the investment managers
agree that all of its officers, employees and agents who
participate in making investment decisions with respect to the
fund will comply with applicable state conflict-of-interest
disclosure requirements and those of the KCERA.
In the Investment Goals, Objectives and Policies Manual
(revised November 1993) that you submitted with your request for
advice, listed as investment managers responsible for a portion of
the assets of the KCERA are:
Equitable Capital,
Fisher
Investments, Newbold’s Asset Management,
Parametric Portfolio
Associates Inc.,
Sun Bank Investment Management Group, Wells Fargo
Investment Advisors,
UBS Phillips
& Drew International Investment,
Wardley Investment Services International, Wells Fargo Nikko
Investment Advisors, Mellon Bond Associates, Fiduciary Trust
Company,
Worldlnvest Limited, Fiduciary Trust Company
International,
Grubb
& Ellis Company,
and FFCA International
Advisors.
Each invests a portion of the KCERA’s assets and each
is responsible for a different aspect of the KCERA’s investment
plan.
Wilshire Associates Incorporated (Wilshire)
Wilshire is an investment consulting firm retained by the
KCERA to assist the retirement administrator.
The employees of
Wilshire do not make any decisions with respect to the investment
of funds.
According to the agreement between Wilshire and the
KCERA, the duties of Wilshire’s employees are as follows:
1.
Annual review of the retirement fund financial condition;
2.
Annual review of the asset allocation plan;
3.
Review of investment policies and guidelines annually or
more often at the KCERA board’s request;
4.
Annual review of investment structure of the retirement
fund;
5.
Provide assistance to the board in selection of
investment managers at the board’s request;
6.
Provide quarterly and annual performance reports
monitoring performance of investment managers and overall
performance of the retirement fund;
7.
Provide “universe” comparisons of the retirement fund’s
performance as compared with other public pension systems;
8.
Provide “universe” comparisons of the performance of the
investment managers as compared with other investment managers
managing the same class of asset and with similar investment
philosophies;


Our File No.
1—95-045
9.
Assist the board in negotiating fees for investment
managers, whether performance—based or otherwise;
10.
Monitor transactions made by investment managers on a
semiannual basis for the purpose of determining the amount of
commissions paid and whether transactions are accomplished in a
manner consistent with the board’s investment policies and
guidelines;
11.
Document facts and analysis which form the basis of
advice provided to the board;
12.
Attend all board meetings in which investments and
issues relating to investments are to be discussed and such other
meetings
(including meetings by standing committees of the board)
where advice and input is requested; and,
13.
Perform such other services and provide reports on such
other investment issues that may be requested by the board or
staff from time to time.
When it is necessary and appropriate, the employees of
Wilshire confer and coordinate with the KCERA’s actuaries in order
to advise the KCERA with respect to future plan obligations and an
appropriate investment plan which will enable the KCERA to meet
those obligations.
You stated that Wilshire has no authority over
the conduct of the investment managers.
Currently, all members of the KCERA board, all natural
persons who advise the board in the capacity of investment counsel
(including employees of Wilshire and the investment managers),
and
the retirement administrator are designated in the conflict of
interest code of the KCER.
They must disclose investments in,
and income from, business entities which are or may foreseeably be
invested in by the KCERA and investments in,
and income from,
persons or business entities engaged in buying and selling
securities for the account of the KCERA.
sections.analysis:
The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California
by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, the Act requires
that assets and income of public officials which may be materially
affected by their official actions should be fully disclosed and
in appropriate circumstances the officials should be disqualified
from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.
(Section 81002(c).)


Our File No.
1-95—045
1.
Other Public Officials who Manage Public Investments
You have asked whether any employees of Wilshire or your
investment managers qualify as filers under Section 87200 and
Regulation 18720.
Regulation 18720(b)
provides that “other public
officials who manage public investments,” as that term is used in
Section 87200, means:
(1)
Members of boards and commissions,
including pension and retirement boards or
commissions, or of committees thereof,
who exercise
responsibility for the management of public
investments;
(2)
High—level officers and employees of
public agencies who exercise primary responsibility
for the management of public investments,
such as
chief or principal investment officers or chief
financial managers.
This category shall not
include officers and employees who work under the
supervision of the chief or principal investment
officers or the chief financial managers;
and
(3)
Individuals who, pursuant to a contract
with a state or local government agency, perform
the same or substantially all the same functions
that would otherwise be performed by the public
officials described in subdivision
(b) (2)
above..
Subdivision
(b) (1)
applies to members of the board itself.
As you noted,
the board members exercise responsibility for the
management of public investments
3 and therefore qualify as “other
public officials who manage public investments,” as that term is
used in Section 87200.
Regulation 18720(a) (3)
defines the “management of public
investments” as the following nonministerial functions:
directing
the investment of public moneys; formulating or approving
investment policies; approving or establishing guidelines for
asset allocations; or approving investment transactions.
“Public investments” means the investment of public moneys in
real estate, securities,
or other economic interests for the
production of revenue or other financial return.
(Regulation
18720(a) (1).)
“Public moneys” means all moneys belonging to,
received by,
or held by, the state,
or any city,
county,
town,
district,
or public agency therein, or by an officer thereof
acting in his or her official capacity,
and includes the proceeds
of all bonds and other evidences of indebtedness, trust funds held
by public pension and retirement systems, deferred compensation
funds held for investment by public agencies,
and public moneys
held by a financial institution under a trust indenture to which a
public agency is a party.
(Regulation 18720 (a) (2).)


Our File No.
1—95-045
Subdivision
(b) (2) applies to high—level officers and
employees of the agency who exercise primary responsibility for
the management of public investments,
such as chief or principal
investment officers or chief financial managers.
Regulation 18720(b) (3) provides that individuals who,
pursuant to a contract with a state or local government agency,
perform the same or substantially all the same functions that
would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in
subdivision
(b) (2) qualify as “other public officials who manage
public investments.”
Thus, this provision would apply to high-
level officers and employees of the contracting firm who exercise
primary responsibility for the management of public investments,
such as chief or principal investment officers or chief financial
managers.
Investment Managers:
According to the material you provided in
connection with your request for advice, the KCERA delegates the
investment management of assets to the investment managers.
This
would constitute the “management of public investments” as defined
in Regulation 18720(a) (3).
Moreover, within the confines of guidelines established by
the board of the KCERA, the investment managers have full
discretion to direct and manage the investment and reinvestment of
assets and may direct trades to specific brokers and receive
research or other services which are of benefit to plan
participants in the management of the plan’s assets.
However, it does not appear from your facts that any of the
investment managers act as chief or principal investment officers
or the chief financial managers for the KCERA.
It appears that
each of the 15 investment managers in question has their authority
limited to a share of the KCERA’s assets and to the types of
investments the manager has contracted to utilize,
and that there
is no individual fulfilling this chief investment manager
function.
Thus, the investment managers would not fall within the
scope of Regulation 18720.
Wilshire:
According to your facts, the employees of Wilshire do
not direct the investment of public moneys into any specific
securities,
formulate or approve the investment policies of the
KCERA,
approve or establish guidelines for asset allocations,
or
approve investment transactions,
as set forth in the definition of
the “management of public investments” in Regulation 18720(a) (3).
Thus, the employees of Wilshire would not be subject to Section
87200,
as interpreted by Regulation l8720.
As noted previously,
Commission advice is based on the facts
provided by the requestor.
If Wilshire does,
in fact, perform
these functions,
you should contact us for further advice.


Our File No.
1—95—045
2.
Consultants
Of course,
employees of these firms may still be
“consultants” and have filing obligations pursuant to the conflict
of interest code of the KCERA.
The comment to Regulation 18720
provides that individuals not coming within the definition
contained in Regulation 18720(b)
may be subject to the conflict-
of—interest disclosure requirements of their agency’s conflict of
interest code.
If this is the case, those individuals must file a
statement of economic interests
(Form 730).
(See also, Regulation
18700(a)
and Section 82019.)
In April 1994, Regulation 18700(a) (2)
was amended to provide
a new definition of “consultant.”
Under the new definition,
an
individual is a consultant if, pursuant to a contract, the
individual:
(A)
Makes a governmental decision whether to:
(i)
Approve a rate, rule,
or regulation;
(ii)
Adopt or enforce a law;
(iii)
Issue,
deny,
suspend,
or revoke
any permit,
license, application, certificate,
approval,
order,
or similar authorization or
entitlement;
(iv)
Authorize the agency to enter into,
modify,
or renew a contract provided it is the
type of contract which requires agency
approval;
(v)
Grant agency approval to a contract
which requires agency approval and in which
the agency is a party or to the specifications
for such a contract;
(vi)
Grant agency approval to a plan,
design, report,
study,
or similar item;
(vii)
Adopt,
or grant agency approval
of, policies, standards, or guidelines for the
agency,
or for any subdivision thereof; or
(B)
Serves in a staff capacity with the
agency and in that capacity performs the same or
substantially all the same duties for the agency
that would otherwise be performed by an individual
holding a position specified in the agency’s
Conflict of Interest Code.
Investment Managers:
While the investment managers are not within
the scope of Regulation 18720, they are clearly “consultants”


Our Page
9
under the definition in Regulation 18700.
According to the
agreement, the investment managers make decisions concerning the
investment of public funds and may direct trades to specific
brokers and agree to a broker’s fee.
They may also accept higher
fees and accept research or other services from the broker in
exchange.
6
These are decisions that would be customarily made by
the board itself or its employees.
Thus,
in essence, the power of
the board to enter into investment contracts has been delegated to
the investment managers.
For these reasons, the employees of the
investment managers who provide services to the KCERA are
currently designated in the conflict of interest code and should
continue to be designated as consultants and subject to the
provisions of the Act.
Of course,
not all employees of the investment managers are
considered consultants.
However, those persons with influence
over the investment decisions pertaining to the KCERA funds would
appear to be consultants under Regulation 18700(a) (2).
Wilshire:
According to your facts, the employees of Wilshire do
not make any of the decisions set forth in subdivision
(a) (2) (A)
above.
However, it appears that Wilshire employees may serve in a
staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity perform the
same or substantially all the same duties for the agency that
would otherwise be performed by an individual holding a position
specified in the agency’s conflict of interest code.
The test established in Regulation 18700 (a) (2) (B)
is a two
fold test, both of which must be satisfied for an individual to
qualify as a consultant.
“The first prong, the ‘staff capacity’
language, eliminates
in most cases from the scope of the regulation those individuals
who work on one project or a limited range of projects for an
agency.
In addition, there is a temporal element to the
qualifier.” ( March 28,
1994 memorandum to the Commission
regarding Regulation 18700, pages
3 and 4.)
Since the adoption of amended Regulation 18700(a) (2),
we have
had one opportunity to interpret the provision.
In the Lyion
Advice Letter,
No.
A-94-353,
we concluded that employees of firms
6
The sample agreement submitted with your request provides that
“the Manager, acting as KCERA’s agent and attorney—in—fact, shall
have discretionary investment authority, without obtaining the
consent of or consulting with the Board or any other person; to
issue to brokers instructions to sell,
and otherwise trade in or
deal with any asset in the Account; to sell to any person any
assets in the Account; to instruct any trustee or custodian of any
asset of the Account to deliver securities or other assets sold,
exchanged,
or otherwise disposed of from the Account and generally
to perform any other act necessary or proper to enable Manager to
carry out its obligations. . ..“


Our Page 10
that were retained to assist the city in a general plan update did
not meet the requirements of Regulation 18700(a) (2) (B).
While the
contractor had primary responsibility for the general plan
preparation, the contractor was only making recommendations
limited to the specific project.
Thus,
we advised that none of
the individuals served in a staff capacity with the agency or
performed the same or substantially all the same duties for the
agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual
designated in the agency’s conflict of interest code.
Conversely, pursuant to the agreement with Wilshire,
employees of the firm attend all board meetings in which
investments and issues relating to investments are to be discussed
and such other meetings (including meetings by standing committees
of the KCERA board) at which advice and input is requested.
In
addition, the employees of Wilshire are required to perform such
other services and provide reports on such other investment issues
that may be requested by the KCERA board or staff from time to
time.
Unlike in the Lyion letter, it appears that the employees
of Wilshire serve on an ongoing basis as staff for the KCERA and
in that capacity participate in the making of all investment
decisions.
“The second prong of the test adds an additional condition:
the tasks of the quasi-staff member over this period of time must
be substantially the same as one of the individuals whose position
at the agency is described in the Conflict of Interest Code.”
( March 28,
1994 memorandum to the Commission regarding
Regulation 18700,.)
Section 87300 provides:
Every agency shall adopt and promulgate a
Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the
provisions of this article.
A Conflict of Interest
Code shall have the force of law and any violation
of a Conflict of Interest Code by a designated
employee shall be deemed a violation of this
chapter.
Section 87302 requires that every conflict of interest code
must specifically enumerate the positions within the agency which
involve the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest.
A conflict of interest code is necessarily a fluid document
with positions being added,
amended and deleted on a periodic
basis.
In fact, Section 87306.5 requires that the code reviewing
body must have the conflict of interest code reviewed every two
years to update its provisions and positions designated therein.
Moreover,
Section 87309 prohibits a code reviewing body from
approving a conflict of interest code or amendment to a conflict
of interest code if it fails to do any of the following:


Our Page 11
(a)
Provide reasonable assurance that all
foreseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented;
(b)
Provide to each affected person a clear
and specific statement of his duties under the
Code; or
(c)
Adequately differentiate between
designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.
Wilshire employees prepare and present reports or opinions
which require the exercise of judgment on their part
(as set forth
in Regulation 18700(c) (2) (B))
and for the purpose of influencing a
governmental decision referenced in subdivision
(a) (2) (A) ——namely,
board decisions to enter into, modify,
or renew contracts with
investment managers,
as well as decisions regarding policies,
standards,
and guidelines for the agency.
(Regulation
18700(a) (2) (A) (iv),
(v),
(vi)
and (vii).)
These types of services constitute the “participation” in
decisions and if the same services were performed by an employee
of the agency, the employee would be designated in the conflict of
interest code.
7
For these reasons, the employees of Wilshire who
provide services to the KCERA are currently designated in the
conflict of interest code and should continue to be designated as
consultants and subject to the provisions of the Act.
Disclosure Categories:
We note that the current disclosure
obligations of Wilshire employees and those of the investment
managers has been limited to investments in,
and income from,
business entities which are or may foreseeably be invested in by
the KCERA and investments in,
and income from, persons or business
entities engaged in buying and selling securities for the account
of the KCERA.
We interpret the language in your conflict of
interest code to require disclosure of all interests in any
business entity that may cause a conflict of interest.
As noted above,
Section 87302 requires that every conflict of
interest code include positions within the agency that involve the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.
An
individual that advises or makes recommendations to the
decisioninaker either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, either by conducting research or making any
investigation for the purpose of influencing the decisionmaker or
preparing or presenting any report, analysis,
or opinion, orally,
or in writing for the purpose of influencing a governmental
decision is participating in making a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700 (c).)


Our File No.
1—95—045
Unlike those persons required to file under Regulation 18720
and who are required to disclose all economic interests,
a
tailored disclosure category in the conflict of interest code is
appropriate so long as this disclosure provides reasonable
assurance that all foreseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented.
Please note, however, that under some circumstances greater
disclosure may be necessary, consistent with Section 87309.
Of
course this will depend on the nature of the powers delegated to
the investment managers and the contractual limits placed on their
power to invest.
3.
The County’s Legal Obligations
As noted above,
a code reviewing body for the KCERA, Section
87309 provides that the county may not approve a conflict of
interest code or amendment to a conflict of interest code if it
fails to do any of the following:
(a)
Provide reasonable assurance that all
foreseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented;
(b)
Provide to each affected person a clear
and specific statement of his duties under the
Code; or
(c)
Adequately differentiate between
designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.
Section 87302 requires that every conflict of interest code
must specifically enumerate the positions within the agency which
involve the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest.
In addition, the conflict of interest code shall
contain requirements that each designated employee shall file
statements disclosing reportable investments, business positions,
interests in real property and income,
limited to those that may
be affected by the employee’s decisionmaking.
Moreover, pursuant to Section 81010, filing officers are
required to:
(a)
Supply the necessary forms and manuals
prescribed by the Commission;
(b)
Determine whether required documents have
been filed and, if so, whether they conform on
their face with the requirements of this title;


Our File No.
1-95—045
(c)
Notify promptly all persons and known
committees who have failed to file a report or
statement in the form and at the time required by
this title;
(d)
Report apparent violations of this title
to the appropriate agencies;
and
(e)
Compile and maintain a current list of
all reports and statements filed with this office.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Are any employees of the various investment management
firms that contract with the KCERA required to file statements of
economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of
interest code of the KCERA?
; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—95—045
2.
Are any employees of Wilshire Associates Inc.
(the
investment consultant to the KCERA)
required to file statements of
economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of
interest code of the KCERA?
3.
What are the county’s legal obligations with respect to
ensuring that individuals file statements of economic interests?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Section 87200,
as interpreted by Regulation 18720(b) (3),
would not apply to the employees of the investment management
firms.
However, since the employees of the investment managers
make decisions regarding the investment of public funds that would
otherwise have to be made by a public agency, they are
“consultants.”
Thus, the employees of the investment managers who
are responsible for investing the funds of the KCERA are public
officials with filing obligations pursuant to the KCERA’s conflict
of interest code.
2.
You stated that the employees of Wilshire do not direct
the investment of public moneys,
formulate or approve the
investment policies of the KCERA,
approve or establish guidelines
for asset allocations,
or approve investment transactions, as set
forth in the definition of “management of public investments” in
Regulation 18720(a) (3).
However,
individuals who serve on an ongoing basis with an
agency and perform the same or substantially all the same duties
for the agency that would be performed by an individual whose
position would be listed in the agency’s conflict of interest code
are “consultants.”
Thus, the employees of Wilshire Associates
Inc. that are responsible for providing these services to the
KCERA are “consultants” and must file statements of economic
interests pursuant to the conflict of interest code of the KCERA.
3.
The county’s conflict of interest code must:
(1)
provide
reasonable assurance that all potential conflicts of interest will
be disclosed or prevented;
(2) provide to each affected person a
clear and specific statement of his or her duties under the
conflict of interest code;
and
(3)
adequately differentiate
between designated employees with different powers and
responsibilities.
In addition to other duties, filing officers must:
(1)
determine whether required documents have been filed;
(2)
determine whether the documents conform with the requirements
of the Act;
(3)
notify persons who have fajied to file;
and
(4) report apparent violations.


Our File No.
1-95—045
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission March 27, 1995 Patricia J. Randolph Deputy County Counsel County of Kern Administrative Center 1115 Truxtun Avenue Bakersfield, CA 93301 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-95-045 Dear Ms. Randolph: This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of the Kern County Employees' Retirement Association (the KCERA) regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). We are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. Are any employees of the various investment management firms that contract with the KCERA required to file statements of economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of interest code of the KCERA? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-95-045 Page 2 2. Are any employees of Wilshire Associates Inc. (the investment consultant to the KCERA) required to file statements of economic interests under either Section 87200 or the conflict of interest code of the KCERA? 3. What are the county's legal obligations with respect to ensuring that individuals file statements of economic interests? CONCLUSIONS 1. Section 87200, as interpreted by Regulation 18720(b)(3), would not apply to the employees of the investment management firms. However, since the employees of the investment managers make decisions regarding the investment of public funds that would otherwise have to be made by a public agency, they are "consultants." Thus, the employees of the investment managers who are responsible for investing the funds of the KCERA are public officials with filing obligations pursuant to the KCERA's conflict of interest code. 2. You stated that the employees of Wilshire do not direct the investment of public moneys, formulate or approve the investment policies of the KCERA, approve or establish guidelines for asset allocations, or approve investment transactions, as set forth in the definition of "management of public investments" in Regulation 18720(a)(3). However, individuals who serve on an ongoing basis with an agency and perform the same or substantially all the duties for the agency that would be performed by an individual whose position would be listed in the agency's conflict of interest code are "consultants." Thus, the employees of Wilshire Associates Inc. that are responsible for providing these services to the KCERA
